frontmatter.title: Initial Vehicle Sizing by Constraint Analysis
frontmatter.subject: Aerospace Engineering

children.value: The correct sizing of an airplane depends on numerous important variables. A clearly stated mission plays a paramount role in this respect and allows the sizing to be accomplished using mathematical tools. Constraint analysis is used to assess the relative significance of selected aircraft performance parameters on the design with respect to a set of requirements.
Specifically, it allows one to understand which combinations of wing loading (

children.html: W/SW/SW/S
P/WP/WP/W

children.value: ) permit the aircraft to simultaneously meet dissimilar performance requirements, such as rate of climb, take-off distance, and others.
Given mission requirements, we perform a constraint analysis using a constraint diagram. From this, we select design parameter values such as:

children.html: VsV_sVs​
VcrV_{cr}Vcr​

children.value: The design space is that region within which all the constraints are met. For each quantitative requirement of the mission, we find the relationship between the wing loading and the power loading that exactly satisfies that requirement.
The general form of the Constraint Equation is:

children.label: eq:constraint-general
children.html: (PW)Sea−Level=f(WS)\left( \frac{P}{W} \right)_{Sea-Level} = f\left( \frac{W}{S} \right)(WP​)Sea−Level​=f(SW​)

children.value: For each requirement, we find the region where the vehicle violates that requirement and indicate the forbidden region with a hash mark. The feasible region (where we want to design our aircraft) is the intersection of all allowed regions. We typically prefer smaller engines (lower

children.html: P/WP/WP/W
W/SW/SW/S

children.value: ), so the optimal point often lies at a corner of the feasible space.
To satisfy the stall speed requirement, the aircraft must be able to generate enough lift to balance its weight at stall velocity

children.value: This is a vertical line on the constraint diagram. Any wing loading greater than this value will violate the stall speed requirement.
Assuming the aircraft is in level flight during cruise, the power required (

children.value: Cruise speeds for propeller-driven airplanes are typically calculated at 75 to 80 percent maximum power. Often, induced drag is small compared to profile drag, giving:

children.label: eq:cruise-drag
children.html: CD=CD0+0.1CD0=1.1CD0C_D = C_{D_0} + 0.1 C_{D_0} = 1.1 C_{D_0}CD​=CD0​​+0.1CD0​​=1.1CD0​​

children.value: If cruise is at sea-level, the constraint equation is:

children.label: eq:cruise-constraint
children.html: PW≥1.1ρVcr3CD02ηp(WS)\frac{P}{W} \ge \frac{1.1 \rho V_{cr}^3 C_{D_0}}{2 \eta_p \left( \frac{W}{S} \right)}WP​≥2ηp​(SW​)1.1ρVcr3​CD0​​​

children.value: This equation creates a curve on the constraint diagram where power loading must be greater than or equal to the required power loading for a given wing loading.

Based on Sadraey’s derivation (Chapter 4), the power loading constraint is:

\frac{W}{P} \le \frac{\eta_P}{V_{to}} \left[ \frac{1 - \exp\left( \frac{0.6 g \rho C_{D_g} S_{to}}{W/S} \right)}{\mu - \left( \mu + \frac{C_{D_g}}{C_{L_g}} \right) \exp\left( \frac{0.6 g \rho C_{D_g} S_{to}}{W/S} \right)} \right]
